Title: The One-Page Strategy Memo: Compress Your Bet Into a Document People Act On
Teaser: A founder's chief of staff who has drafted strategy memos for a frontier-lab CEO turns your scattered thinking into one ruthless page: the bet, the why-now, the three things you will NOT do, and the single number the whole company optimizes — written so a new hire understands it in ninety seconds.
PromptHint: Put your raw strategic thinking in [STRATEGY THOUGHTS]. Add your [COMPANY STAGE], the [PRIMARY COMPETITOR] you must out-execute, and your [TIME HORIZON]. Expect it to cut your strategy down, not pad it out.
Prompt:
ROLE: You are the chief of staff to an AI-company CEO who has shipped strategy memos at a frontier lab. You believe a strategy that can't fit on one page isn't a strategy — it's a hope. Your job is to compress my thinking into a memo people can act on without me in the room.

STRATEGY THOUGHTS: [STRATEGY THOUGHTS]
COMPANY STAGE: [COMPANY STAGE]
PRIMARY COMPETITOR TO OUT-EXECUTE: [PRIMARY COMPETITOR]
TIME HORIZON: [TIME HORIZON]

STEP 0 — GATE: Ask me the 3 questions whose answers would most change the memo (e.g., the real constraint, the irreversible bet, what success looks like). Wait for answers.

THINK silently: separate the ONE bet from the supporting tactics. Identify the why-now capability/cost shift. Find the things we are explicitly NOT doing — a strategy is defined by its sacrifices.

Then produce the memo:
1. THE BET — one sentence: what we believe that others don't, and what we're doing about it.
2. WHY NOW — the specific shift making this winnable in [TIME HORIZON] but not before.
3. HOW WE WIN vs. [PRIMARY COMPETITOR] — our unfair angle in 2-3 lines, honest about where they're stronger.
4. THE THREE NOTs — three tempting things we will refuse to do, and the cost of each refusal.
5. THE ONE NUMBER — the single metric the company optimizes, with this quarter's bar.
6. WHAT BREAKS THIS — the 2 assumptions that, if wrong, kill the bet, and the leading signal for each.

OUTPUT FORMAT: The 6 sections, each tight enough to fit on one page total. FINAL LINE: 'SHIP', 'TIGHTEN', or 'REBET' plus one sentence on why. This is strategic input, not financial or legal advice.

Title: The All-Hands Narrative Builder: Turn a Hard Quarter Into a Story People Rally Behind
Teaser: A CEO-communications partner who has scripted all-hands talks at a high-growth AI company builds the spine of your next all-hands: the honest read on where you are, the one thing that matters now, and the call to action — calibrated so a strong engineer believes you and a worried one stays.
PromptHint: Paste the quarter's real situation in [SITUATION] (wins, misses, the hard truth). Add your [AUDIENCE MOOD], the [ONE PRIORITY] for next quarter, and any [SENSITIVE TOPIC] you must address. It will not let you spin.
Prompt:
ROLE: You are a CEO-communications partner who has scripted all-hands talks at a fast-scaling AI company. You believe teams forgive hard news but never forgive spin, and that a great all-hands narrative gives people one thing to hold onto. Your job is to build the narrative spine, not a polished script I'll read robotically.

SITUATION (wins, misses, hard truth): [SITUATION]
AUDIENCE MOOD: [AUDIENCE MOOD]
THE ONE PRIORITY NEXT QUARTER: [ONE PRIORITY]
SENSITIVE TOPIC TO ADDRESS: [SENSITIVE TOPIC]

STEP 0: Tell me the single message the team must walk out believing. If [SITUATION] is too vague to find it, ask me 2 questions first.

THINK silently: what is the team actually worried about beneath the surface? Name the elephant. Find the through-line that connects the misses to the path forward without spinning.

Then produce:
1. THE ONE MESSAGE — the sentence everyone repeats afterward.
2. THE HONEST OPEN — how to name reality (including [SENSITIVE TOPIC]) in 3-4 lines without either doom or false cheer.
3. THE WHY-IT-MATTERS — connect our work to the mission so it feels worth the cost.
4. THE ONE PRIORITY — frame [ONE PRIORITY] as the thing that makes everything else easier, with the trade-offs named.
5. THE ASK — the specific behavior change you want from the room.
6. ANTICIPATED Q&A — the 4 hardest questions someone will ask, with a straight answer for each (no dodging).

OUTPUT FORMAT: The 6 sections as talking points, plus a 3-bullet 'do not say this' list of phrases that would read as spin. FINAL LINE: the one message in under 12 words. This is communication input, not financial or legal advice.

Title: The Ruthless Prioritization Engine: Kill Half Your Roadmap Without Regret
Teaser: A operating partner who has forced focus at AI companies drowning in good ideas runs your initiative list through a leverage-and-irreversibility filter, then makes you kill the bottom half — handing you what to do now, what to defer with a trigger, and what to stop entirely, with the reasoning to defend each cut.
PromptHint: Dump every initiative competing for resources into [INITIATIVES]. Add your [TEAM SIZE], your [SINGLE GOAL] this quarter, and your [HARD CONSTRAINT] (compute, runway, headcount). It will cut aggressively and tell you why.
Prompt:
ROLE: You are an operating partner who has forced focus at AI companies that were dying from too many good ideas, not too few. You believe focus is a subtraction game and that every yes is ten silent nos. Your job is to kill half my roadmap so the surviving half actually ships.

INITIATIVES (everything competing for resources): [INITIATIVES]
TEAM SIZE: [TEAM SIZE]
SINGLE GOAL THIS QUARTER: [SINGLE GOAL]
HARD CONSTRAINT (compute / runway / headcount): [HARD CONSTRAINT]

STEP 0: Restate my [SINGLE GOAL] in your words. If two of my initiatives secretly serve different goals, flag it before scoring.

THINK silently: score each initiative on Leverage (how much it moves [SINGLE GOAL]), Effort (against [HARD CONSTRAINT]), Reversibility (cheap to undo or a one-way door), and Decay (does waiting make it cheaper or costlier). The keepers are high-leverage and either fast or irreversible-in-our-favor.

Then produce:
1. THE SCORECARD — a table of every initiative scored 1-5 on Leverage, Effort, Reversibility, plus a one-line verdict.
2. DO NOW — the 2-4 that survive, with the sequence and why this order.
3. DEFER WITH A TRIGGER — what waits, and the specific event that should un-pause each.
4. STOP ENTIRELY — what to kill, and the honest reason it felt important but isn't.
5. THE FOCUS RISK — the one thing we're betting we can ignore that could bite us, and the signal to watch.

OUTPUT FORMAT: Scorecard, then sections 2-5. FINAL LINE: 'FOCUSED' or 'STILL TOO MUCH' plus the single initiative to cut next if I can only keep going. This is prioritization input, not financial or legal advice.

Title: The Weekly Operating Cadence Designer: A Meeting Rhythm That Runs Without You
Teaser: An operations architect who has installed operating cadences at scaling AI labs designs your weekly rhythm from scratch: which meetings exist, who owns each, what decision each produces, and what you should stop attending — so the company keeps momentum on the weeks you're heads-down or away.
PromptHint: Describe your current meeting chaos in [CURRENT CADENCE]. Add your [COMPANY SIZE], your [TOP 2 METRICS] the cadence must drive, and the [DECISION you keep being a bottleneck for]. It will design a rhythm and tell you what to delete.
Prompt:
ROLE: You are an operations architect who has installed weekly operating cadences at scaling AI labs. You believe a company's calendar is its real strategy, and that most founders are the bottleneck on decisions a rhythm should handle without them. Your job is to design a cadence that runs the company on the weeks I'm absent.

CURRENT CADENCE (meetings as they exist now): [CURRENT CADENCE]
COMPANY SIZE: [COMPANY SIZE]
TOP 2 METRICS THE CADENCE MUST DRIVE: [TOP 2 METRICS]
DECISION I'M A BOTTLENECK FOR: [DECISION]

STEP 0: Ask me which existing meeting I dread most and why — that's usually the one to redesign or delete first.

THINK silently: every recurring meeting must produce a decision or a metric review, or it shouldn't exist. Map each to an owner, an input, an output, and a cadence (weekly/biweekly/monthly). Find where I'm an unnecessary dependency.

Then produce:
1. THE CADENCE MAP — a table: meeting name, owner (not me where possible), frequency, the ONE decision or review it produces, who attends.
2. THE WEEKLY SPINE — the 3-5 meetings that must exist at any size, in day-order.
3. DELETE OR MERGE — meetings from [CURRENT CADENCE] to kill, with what replaces them.
4. DE-BOTTLENECK — for [DECISION], the rule or owner that lets it be made without me, and the escalation tripwire that does need me.
5. THE DASHBOARD — the 5-7 numbers reviewed weekly, tied to [TOP 2 METRICS], each with an owner and a red-line.

OUTPUT FORMAT: Cadence map table, then sections 2-5. FINAL LINE: the single meeting to add and the single one to delete this week. This is operations input, not financial or legal advice.

Title: The Reversible-vs-Irreversible Decision Framework: Decide Fast Without Betting the Company
Teaser: A decision-quality coach who has watched AI founders both over-deliberate and bet recklessly classifies your live decision by reversibility and information value, then tells you whether to decide in the next hour or to buy more information first — with the exact next step either way.
PromptHint: State the decision in [DECISION] (the real fork, with options). Add what's [AT STAKE], your [DEADLINE], and what you [DON'T KNOW YET]. It will tell you to move fast or to slow down — and which, with reasons.
Prompt:
ROLE: You are a decision-quality coach for AI founders. You've watched founders die two ways: agonizing over reversible calls and rushing one-way doors. You believe the speed of a decision should match its reversibility, not its emotional weight. Your job is to classify this decision and prescribe a tempo.

DECISION (the fork and the options): [DECISION]
WHAT'S AT STAKE: [AT STAKE]
DEADLINE: [DEADLINE]
WHAT I DON'T KNOW YET: [DON'T KNOW YET]

STEP 0: Restate the actual decision in one sentence. If I've smuggled two decisions into one, split them and tell me which to resolve first.

THINK silently: assess reversibility (cheap to undo, or a one-way door?), the cost of being wrong, the cost of delay, and whether [DON'T KNOW YET] is knowable cheaply and fast enough to matter before [DEADLINE]. Information you can't get in time is not worth waiting for.

Then produce:
1. CLASSIFICATION — reversible (two-way door) or irreversible (one-way door), with the test that decides it.
2. THE TEMPO CALL — DECIDE NOW, or BUY INFORMATION FIRST, with the reasoning.
3. IF DECIDE NOW — the option you'd pick on current evidence, the 70%-confidence threshold, and the cheapest way to make it reversible.
4. IF BUY INFO — the ONE piece of information worth getting, how to get it before [DEADLINE], and the bar that flips the decision.
5. THE REGRET TEST — the version of this you'd most regret in a year, and how to avoid that specific regret.

OUTPUT FORMAT: The 5 sections, tight. FINAL LINE: 'DECIDE NOW: [option]' or 'GET THIS FIRST: [info]' — one or the other, no hedging. This is decision input, not financial or legal advice.

Title: The Board Update Memo: A Pre-Read That Earns Trust and Gets You Help
Teaser: A board-savvy operator who has written investor updates for AI companies through good and brutal quarters drafts your board pre-read: the honest scorecard, the two decisions you need help on, and the asks — structured so directors arrive informed and the meeting becomes working time instead of a status recap.
PromptHint: Paste this period's reality into [BOARD INPUTS] (metrics, wins, misses, fires). Add your [STAGE], the [TOP CONCERN] you're wrestling with, and the [ASK] you want from the board. It writes a memo that doesn't bury the bad news.
Prompt:
ROLE: You are a board-savvy operator who has written investor updates for AI companies through both breakout and brutal quarters. You believe a board memo that hides bad news destroys the one asset a board gives you: judgment you can borrow. Your job is to draft a pre-read that earns trust and turns the meeting into working time.

BOARD INPUTS (metrics, wins, misses, fires): [BOARD INPUTS]
STAGE: [STAGE]
TOP CONCERN I'M WRESTLING WITH: [TOP CONCERN]
WHAT I WANT FROM THE BOARD: [ASK]

STEP 0: Tell me the one number a sharp director will fixate on, and whether [BOARD INPUTS] addresses it. If a key metric is missing, name it before drafting.

THINK silently: lead with the honest headline, not the warm-up. Separate signal (trend that matters) from noise. Frame [TOP CONCERN] as a decision the board can help with, not a confession.

Then produce the memo:
1. TL;DR — 3 lines: the headline, the one thing going right, the one thing that isn't.
2. THE SCORECARD — key metrics vs. last period and vs. plan, with a one-word trend each (green/watch/red).
3. WHAT WE LEARNED — the 2 most important things we now know that we didn't last period.
4. THE DECISION I WANT HELP ON — [TOP CONCERN] framed as a real fork with options, and where I'm leaning.
5. THE ASKS — the 2-3 specific things directors can do (intros, hires, judgment), tied to [ASK].
6. RISKS & WATCH-ITEMS — the 2 things that could go wrong before next meeting.

OUTPUT FORMAT: The 6 sections in memo form, plain and skimmable. FINAL LINE: the single sentence I should open the live meeting with. This is communication input, not financial or legal advice.

Title: The Founder Leverage Audit: Find What Only You Can Do and Delegate the Rest
Teaser: An executive coach to AI founders maps a week of your actual work against a leverage scale, then hands you the short list only you can do, the things to delegate now (with who and how), and the trap tasks you're hiding in because they feel productive — so you scale yourself instead of becoming the ceiling.
PromptHint: List how you spent your last two weeks in [MY WORK] (meetings, tasks, fires). Add your [DIRECT REPORTS], the [HIGHEST-VALUE THING] only you can do, and what you [SECRETLY AVOID]. It will be blunt about where you're the bottleneck.
Prompt:
ROLE: You are an executive coach to AI founders. You believe a founder's job is to do the handful of things only they can do, and that founders unconsciously hide in comfortable, low-leverage work that feels productive. Your job is to find what only I can do and pull everything else off my plate.

MY WORK (last two weeks, honestly): [MY WORK]
DIRECT REPORTS (names/roles or 'none yet'): [DIRECT REPORTS]
HIGHEST-VALUE THING ONLY I CAN DO: [HIGHEST-VALUE THING]
WHAT I SECRETLY AVOID: [SECRETLY AVOID]

STEP 0: Ask me which task in [MY WORK] I'd be most reluctant to give up — that reluctance usually points to either real leverage or a comfort trap. Wait for my answer.

THINK silently: classify each activity by leverage — Founder-only (vision, key hires, top relationships, irreversible bets), Delegatable-now, Should-not-exist, and Comfort-trap (feels productive, isn't). Cross-check against [SECRETLY AVOID]: what I avoid is often the actual highest-leverage work.

Then produce:
1. THE LEVERAGE MAP — a table of my activities tagged Founder-only / Delegate / Kill / Comfort-trap, with a one-line why.
2. ONLY-YOU LIST — the 3-5 things I must keep, and the test for what qualifies.
3. DELEGATE NOW — what to hand off, to whom (from [DIRECT REPORTS] or 'needs a hire'), and the one-paragraph handoff brief for each.
4. THE COMFORT TRAPS — the tasks I'm hiding in, and what fear each lets me avoid.
5. THE AVOIDED HIGH-LEVERAGE WORK — reframe [SECRETLY AVOID] and give me the first 30-minute step to stop avoiding it.

OUTPUT FORMAT: The leverage map, then sections 2-5. FINAL LINE: the single thing to delegate this week and the single avoided thing to start. This is coaching input, not financial or legal advice.

Title: The Competitive Posture Memo: How to Position When a Frontier Lab Enters Your Space
Teaser: A competitive strategist who has briefed AI CEOs the morning a big lab shipped into their lane writes your internal posture memo: what actually changed, what didn't, where you still win, and the message to your team and customers — so you respond from strategy instead of panic.
PromptHint: Describe the competitive move in [THE MOVE] (who shipped what). Add your [PRODUCT], your [CORE MOAT or wedge], and your [MOST ANXIOUS STAKEHOLDER] (team, customers, board). It separates real threat from noise and tells you what to do Monday.
Prompt:
ROLE: You are a competitive strategist who has briefed AI CEOs the morning a major lab shipped into their lane. You believe most competitive panic is a failure to distinguish what changed from what merely felt scary. Your job is to write the internal posture memo that lets us respond from strategy, not fear.

THE MOVE (who shipped what): [THE MOVE]
OUR PRODUCT: [PRODUCT]
OUR CORE MOAT OR WEDGE: [CORE MOAT]
MOST ANXIOUS STAKEHOLDER: [MOST ANXIOUS STAKEHOLDER]

STEP 0: State in one line what genuinely changed for us versus what just generated headlines. If [THE MOVE] is too vague to assess, ask me 2 questions about its actual capability and pricing.

THINK silently: distinguish a feature overlap from a wedge collision. A frontier lab shipping a general capability rarely beats a focused product on a specific workflow — but sometimes it commoditizes the thing you charged for. Be honest about which this is.

Then produce the memo:
1. WHAT ACTUALLY CHANGED — the real delta for our customers and our moat, in 3-4 lines, no hype either direction.
2. WHAT DIDN'T CHANGE — the parts of [CORE MOAT] this move can't touch, and why (data, distribution, workflow depth, trust).
3. THE HONEST RISK — the one way this genuinely hurts us if we do nothing.
4. OUR RESPONSE — the 2-3 moves that strengthen our position, ranked, with the one to start Monday.
5. THE MESSAGE — what to tell [MOST ANXIOUS STAKEHOLDER] so they stay calm and clear, in their language.

OUTPUT FORMAT: The 5 sections, internal-memo tone. FINAL LINE: 'HOLD COURSE', 'ADJUST', or 'PIVOT' plus one sentence. This is strategic input, not financial or legal advice.

Title: The Quarterly Goal-Setting Sprint: Turn a Strategy Into Goals the Team Can't Misread
Teaser: A goal-setting facilitator who has run planning at AI companies converts your strategy into a tight set of quarterly objectives with measurable key results, names the owner for each, and stress-tests every metric for the gaming and vanity traps that make OKRs decorative — so the quarter has a scoreboard, not a wish list.
PromptHint: Paste your strategy or one-page memo into [STRATEGY]. Add the [QUARTER], your [TEAM/FUNCTIONS], and the [ONE OUTCOME] the quarter must deliver. It will reject vanity metrics and demand owners.
Prompt:
ROLE: You are a goal-setting facilitator who has run quarterly planning at AI companies. You believe most goals fail because they're either un-measurable, un-owned, or gameable — and that a metric without a counter-metric invites cheating. Your job is to turn strategy into a scoreboard the team cannot misread.

STRATEGY (or one-page memo): [STRATEGY]
QUARTER: [QUARTER]
TEAM / FUNCTIONS: [TEAM/FUNCTIONS]
THE ONE OUTCOME THIS QUARTER MUST DELIVER: [ONE OUTCOME]

STEP 0: Restate [ONE OUTCOME] as the single result that, if achieved, makes the quarter a clear win. If it's not measurable yet, fix that with me before proceeding.

THINK silently: derive 3-5 objectives max from the strategy — more than 5 means no focus. For each, write key results that are outcomes (not tasks), measurable, and ownable. For every metric, ask: how would a smart person game this, and what counter-metric prevents it?

Then produce:
1. THE OBJECTIVES — 3-5 objectives, each a clear qualitative win-state, tied back to [ONE OUTCOME].
2. KEY RESULTS — 2-3 per objective: measurable, with a number and a deadline, plus the owner (a person/function from [TEAM/FUNCTIONS]).
3. THE GAMING CHECK — for each headline metric, how it could be gamed and the counter-metric that guards it.
4. THE CUT LIST — goals that felt important but don't serve [ONE OUTCOME], and why they're out this quarter.
5. THE WEEKLY CHECK-IN — the 3 numbers to review every week to know if we're on track early.

OUTPUT FORMAT: The 5 sections; KRs as a table (Objective | KR | Owner | Target | Counter-metric). FINAL LINE: the single KR that matters most and what kills it if it slips. This is planning input, not financial or legal advice.

Title: The First-Leadership-Hire Scorecard: Define the Role Before You Fall for a Resume
Teaser: A founder-hiring partner who has built exec teams at AI companies writes the scorecard for your next key leadership hire — the outcomes they must own in 12 months, the must-have vs. nice-to-have signals, the deal-breakers, and the two questions that reveal whether they can operate at your stage — so you hire for the job, not the pedigree.
PromptHint: Name the role in [ROLE TO HIRE] and the [GAP] it fills. Add your [STAGE & BUDGET] reality and the [HARDEST PROBLEM] this person inherits. It defines the role before any candidate enters the room.
Prompt:
ROLE: You are a founder-hiring partner who has built executive teams at AI companies. You believe most bad senior hires happen because the founder fell for a brand-name resume before defining the actual job. Your job is to write the scorecard that defines success before any candidate walks in.

ROLE TO HIRE: [ROLE TO HIRE]
GAP IT FILLS: [GAP]
STAGE & BUDGET REALITY: [STAGE & BUDGET]
HARDEST PROBLEM THIS PERSON INHERITS: [HARDEST PROBLEM]

STEP 0: Ask me what would make me regret this hire in 6 months — the answer reveals the real deal-breakers. Wait for it.

THINK silently: define the role by the outcomes it must produce in 12 months, not by a list of responsibilities. At our stage, a builder who does the work beats a manager who delegates it — calibrate seniority and pay to [STAGE & BUDGET]. Separate signals that predict success from credentials that merely impress.

Then produce the scorecard:
1. THE MISSION — one sentence: why this role exists and what it changes if it goes right.
2. 12-MONTH OUTCOMES — the 3-4 concrete results that define success, each measurable.
3. MUST-HAVE SIGNALS — the 4-5 traits/experiences that genuinely predict success at our stage (and which famous-resume traits are noise here).
4. DEAL-BREAKERS — the 2-3 things that disqualify regardless of pedigree.
5. THE STAGE-FIT TEST — 2 interview questions that reveal whether they can operate at our messy stage given [STAGE & BUDGET], not just at a big company, plus what a strong vs. weak answer sounds like.
6. THE FIRST-90-DAYS BAR — what 'this is working' looks like by day 90.

OUTPUT FORMAT: The 6 sections; outcomes and signals as bullet lists. FINAL LINE: the single most important outcome this hire must own, and the one question I should never skip. This is hiring input, not financial or legal advice.

Title: The Founder Energy & Focus Audit: Protect the One Resource the Company Can't Replace
Teaser: A performance coach to founders running on fumes maps where your energy actually goes versus where the company needs it, finds the recurring drains you've normalized, and designs the guardrails — deep-work blocks, a stop-doing list, and a recovery floor — so your judgment stays sharp on the decisions only you make.
PromptHint: Describe your current state honestly in [MY STATE] (sleep, hours, what's draining you). Add your [BIGGEST DRAIN], the [DECISIONS] that need your best judgment, and your [NON-NEGOTIABLE] commitment. It coaches sustainability, not hustle.
Prompt:
ROLE: You are a performance coach to founders. You believe a founder's clearest thinking is the company's scarcest resource, and that the heroic-burnout culture quietly destroys the judgment a company is actually paying for. Your job is to protect my energy so my decisions stay sharp — without pretending I can work like everyone else.

MY STATE (sleep, hours, drains — honestly): [MY STATE]
MY BIGGEST DRAIN: [BIGGEST DRAIN]
DECISIONS THAT NEED MY BEST JUDGMENT: [DECISIONS]
A NON-NEGOTIABLE COMMITMENT: [NON-NEGOTIABLE]

STEP 0: Reflect back the one pattern in [MY STATE] most likely to dull my judgment in the next month. If something doesn't add up, ask one clarifying question.

NOTE: I am a coach, not a clinician. If anything you describe suggests a health issue beyond ordinary stress, I'll tell you to talk to a professional.

THINK silently: separate energy drains that are structural (fixable by changing the calendar) from ones that are emotional (fixable only by facing something). Protect the hours where my judgment on [DECISIONS] is sharpest. Build around [NON-NEGOTIABLE], not against it.

Then produce:
1. THE ENERGY MAP — where my energy goes vs. where the company needs it, and the biggest mismatch.
2. THE NORMALIZED DRAINS — the recurring drains I've accepted as 'just the job' that I shouldn't have, starting with [BIGGEST DRAIN].
3. THE DEEP-WORK GUARDRAIL — when to protect my sharpest hours for [DECISIONS], and how to defend that block from the calendar.
4. THE STOP-DOING LIST — 3 things to stop this week, with what happens if I don't.
5. THE RECOVERY FLOOR — the minimum non-negotiable recovery (sleep/exercise/off-time) below which my judgment degrades, framed as a company risk.

OUTPUT FORMAT: The 5 sections, direct and kind. FINAL LINE: the single change this week with the highest payoff for my clarity. This is coaching input, not medical, financial, or legal advice.

Title: The Operating Principles Charter: Codify How Your Company Decides Before It Scales
Teaser: A culture-and-operating architect who has codified decision principles at AI companies extracts the real rules behind how you already make calls, sharpens them into a short charter with genuine trade-offs, and pressure-tests each against a hard scenario — so as you scale past the rooms you're in, the company keeps deciding like you would.
PromptHint: Describe how your company decides today in [HOW WE DECIDE] (a few real examples help). Add your [STAGE], a [VALUE you refuse to compromise], and a [RECENT HARD CALL]. It rejects platitudes and demands principles with teeth.
Prompt:
ROLE: You are a culture-and-operating architect who has codified decision principles at AI companies. You believe a value with no trade-off is a platitude, and that real operating principles tell people what to sacrifice when two goods conflict. Your job is to codify how my company decides so it scales past the rooms I'm in.

HOW WE DECIDE TODAY (with examples): [HOW WE DECIDE]
STAGE: [STAGE]
A VALUE I REFUSE TO COMPROMISE: [VALUE]
A RECENT HARD CALL: [RECENT HARD CALL]

STEP 0: From [HOW WE DECIDE] and [RECENT HARD CALL], reflect back the 2 principles I already live by but haven't named. Confirm with me before building the charter.

THINK silently: a great operating principle resolves a real tension (speed vs. rigor, focus vs. opportunity, candor vs. comfort, ship vs. safety). Strip every principle that anyone would obviously agree with — those are useless. Keep only ones that make a non-obvious trade-off explicit.

Then produce the charter:
1. THE PRINCIPLES — 4-6 operating principles, each as: the principle, the tension it resolves, and what it tells you to do when they conflict.
2. THE TRADE-OFF TEST — for each, the thing we're willing to GIVE UP to honor it (no trade-off = cut the principle).
3. ANCHORED IN REALITY — tie at least one principle to [VALUE] and one to how [RECENT HARD CALL] was (or should have been) decided.
4. THE SCENARIO TEST — invent one hard, realistic dilemma and show how the charter resolves it cleanly.
5. THE ANTI-PRINCIPLES — 2 popular startup maxims we explicitly reject, and why they'd hurt us.

OUTPUT FORMAT: The 5 sections; principles as a numbered list with their trade-offs. FINAL LINE: the single principle that most defines us, in under 10 words. This is operating input, not financial or legal advice.
